queries.title: Predicate pushdown into the inner join
queries.original_sql:
SELECT u.id, o.total
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE o.created_at >= '2026-01-01'
  AND u.country = 'US';
queries.optimized_sql:
SELECT u.id, o.total
FROM (
  SELECT id
  FROM users
  WHERE country = 'US'
) u
JOIN (
  SELECT user_id, total
  FROM orders
  WHERE created_at >= '2026-01-01'
) o
  ON o.user_id = u.id;

reasoning.text: Push country and date filters into subqueries so each side scans fewer rows before the join.
On Snowflake / Iceberg this lets the engine prune partitions and apply Bloom filters before shuffle.
Typical 3-10x reduction in shuffle bytes on joins with selective filters.

queries.explanation: Filters higher in the plan get pushed down to scan-time, dropping rows before they participate in the join.

queries.title: Broadcast small dimension instead of shuffle join

reasoning.text: products is ~80 MB; transactions is ~2 TB. Broadcasting the small side avoids hashing 2 TB across the cluster.
Cuts network shuffle from ~2 TB to ~80 MB times executor count.
Reduces executor memory pressure; the join becomes a hash lookup per partition.

queries.explanation: Broadcast joins replace a full shuffle with a one-time replication of the small side.

queries.title: Add partition predicate so the planner can prune
queries.optimized_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*)
FROM events
WHERE event_type = 'signup'
  AND event_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL 90 DAY;

reasoning.text: events is partitioned by event_date; without a date predicate the engine scans all partitions.
Adding the 90-day window prunes ~95% of partitions for a year-long table.
Direct cost win on Snowflake (per-byte) and BigQuery (per-byte slot-time).

queries.explanation: The optimizer can only prune partitions when the predicate explicitly references the partition column.

queries.title: Replace self-join with window function
queries.original_sql:
SELECT a.user_id, a.amount, a.amount - b.prev_amount AS delta
FROM payments a
JOIN payments b
  ON b.user_id = a.user_id
 AND b.payment_id = (
    SELECT MAX(payment_id)
    FROM payments p
    WHERE p.user_id = a.user_id
      AND p.payment_id < a.payment_id
  );
queries.optimized_sql:
SELECT user_id,
       amount,
       amount - LAG(amount) OVER (
         PARTITION BY user_id
         ORDER BY payment_id
       ) AS delta
FROM payments;

reasoning.text: The self-join + correlated subquery requires multiple passes; LAG() is one pass with a sort.
Eliminates O(n^2) worst-case behavior on heavy users.
Far more readable and easier to extend (LAG offset, RANGE windows, etc.).

queries.explanation: Window functions are designed for previous/next-row lookups and avoid the cost of self-joins.

queries.title: Project only required columns

reasoning.text: On columnar formats (Parquet / Iceberg / Delta), reading only needed columns skips entire column files.
Avoids materializing wide nested struct columns the consumer never uses.
Smaller projection reduces serialization cost across BI tool boundaries.

queries.explanation: Columnar storage rewards projection pruning. SELECT * defeats every column-pruning optimization.
